description: STRIDE-LM is a threat classification model covering Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege, and Lateral Movement.

entries.name: Identity Spoofing via Credential Theft
entries.description: An attacker steals or guesses user credentials and impersonates a legitimate user or service.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA). Use short-lived tokens and session timeouts. Monitor for unusual login patterns and alert on anomalous access.

entries.name: Service Impersonation (Man-in-the-Middle)
entries.description: An attacker intercepts communications and masquerades as a trusted service, causing clients to connect to a rogue endpoint.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enforce mutual TLS. Use certificate pinning for mobile/internal clients. Deploy HSTS and validate server certificates strictly.

entries.name: DNS Spoofing / Cache Poisoning
entries.description: An attacker manipulates DNS responses to redirect users to a malicious server that impersonates a legitimate service.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Implement DNSSEC. Use encrypted DNS (DoH/DoT). Monitor DNS resolution anomalies via security tooling.

entries.name: Data Tampering in Transit
entries.description: An attacker intercepts and modifies data flowing between components, corrupting data integrity without detection.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enforce TLS 1.2+ for all data in transit. Use HMAC or digital signatures for message integrity. Validate checksums at receiving end.

entries.name: Database Record Manipulation
entries.description: An attacker with database access modifies records directly, bypassing application-level controls.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Restrict direct database access to service accounts only. Enable audit logging on sensitive tables. Use integrity constraints and triggers to detect unexpected changes.

entries.name: Configuration File Tampering
entries.description: An attacker modifies application configuration files to introduce backdoors or disable security controls.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Store configuration in a secrets manager or sealed vault. Restrict file-system write access. Detect unauthorised file changes with a host-based integrity monitor.

entries.name: Log Deletion / Modification
entries.description: An attacker deletes or alters audit logs after a compromise to hide their actions.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Stream logs to an immutable, centralised SIEM in real time. Restrict log write access to the logging agent only. Alert on gaps or unusual log-volume drops.

entries.name: Action Repudiation (Missing Audit Trail)
entries.description: A user denies performing a sensitive action because the system lacks binding audit evidence.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Log all state-changing actions with user identity, timestamp, source IP, and payload hash. Store logs in a tamper-evident store. Include non-repudiation in API design (signed requests).

entries.name: Sensitive Data Exposure via Misconfigured Storage
entries.description: Confidential data (PII, credentials, source code) is publicly accessible due to a misconfigured cloud bucket, server, or repository.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enforce private-by-default policies on object storage. Run automated configuration scanners (e.g. CSPM). Classify data and apply appropriate access controls at creation.

entries.name: Verbose Error Messages Leaking Internals
entries.description: Application error responses expose stack traces, SQL queries, or internal paths, enabling reconnaissance.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Return generic error messages to clients. Log detailed errors server-side only. Disable debug mode in production. Use a structured exception-handling middleware.

entries.name: Side-Channel Information Leakage
entries.description: Timing differences, power consumption, or cache behaviour reveal secret information (e.g. password length, cryptographic key bits).
entries.suggested_mitigation: Use constant-time comparison functions for secrets. Apply padding and timing jitter to sensitive operations. Audit third-party libraries for side-channel vulnerabilities.

entries.name: Resource Exhaustion via Unbounded Input
entries.description: An attacker sends oversized or malformed inputs that exhaust CPU, memory, or disk, rendering the service unavailable.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enforce input size limits and rate limiting at the API gateway. Deploy auto-scaling with resource caps. Use circuit breakers to fail-fast under overload.

entries.name: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)
entries.description: A coordinated flood of traffic from many sources overwhelms network infrastructure or application endpoints.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Deploy a CDN with DDoS mitigation. Use upstream traffic scrubbing services. Implement anycast routing and geographic load distribution.

entries.name: Privilege Escalation via Broken Access Control
entries.description: An attacker exploits missing or misconfigured access controls to gain higher privileges than intended.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Apply the principle of least privilege. Centralise authorisation decisions. Conduct regular privilege audits and automated access control testing.

entries.name: Kernel or Container Escape
entries.description: An attacker escapes from an application sandbox, container, or VM to gain host-level privileges.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Run containers as non-root. Apply kernel security modules (seccomp, AppArmor). Keep hypervisors and container runtimes patched. Use read-only file systems where possible.

entries.name: Lateral Movement via Credential Reuse
entries.description: After compromising one account or service, an attacker reuses credentials to access other systems in the same environment.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enforce unique credentials per service. Adopt just-in-time (JIT) access provisioning. Segment networks and enforce micro-segmentation to limit blast radius.

entries.name: Lateral Movement via Exploited Trust Relationships
entries.description: An attacker leverages implicit trust between internal systems (SSH keys, service tokens) to move laterally without triggering standard alerts.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Audit and rotate all service-to-service credentials regularly. Deploy zero-trust network access (ZTNA). Monitor east-west traffic for anomalous patterns.

description: The OWASP Top 10 (2021) identifies the most critical web application security risks, from access control failures to server-side request forgery.

entries.name: Broken Access Control — Insecure Direct Object Reference
entries.description: A user manipulates a URL parameter or API reference (e.g. `/api/orders/1234`) to access another user's resources without authorisation.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Implement server-side authorisation checks on every resource request. Use indirect references (UUIDs or hashed IDs) where possible. Add automated integration tests for access control boundaries.

entries.name: Broken Access Control — Missing Function-Level Access Control
entries.description: Administrative or privileged functions are accessible to lower-privileged users because the server relies solely on client-side UI hiding.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enforce role-based access control (RBAC) on every server-side function. Deny by default. Use a centralised policy-enforcement point for all privileged actions.

entries.name: Cryptographic Failures — Weak Cipher Suite
entries.description: The application uses outdated algorithms (DES, MD5, SHA-1, RC4) that can be broken to expose sensitive data.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Use TLS 1.2+ with strong cipher suites (e.g. ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384). Migrate hashing to SHA-256 or better. Regularly audit cryptographic libraries and configurations.

entries.name: Cryptographic Failures — Plaintext Sensitive Data Storage
entries.description: Passwords, API keys, or PII are stored in clear text in databases, logs, or configuration files.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Hash passwords with bcrypt/Argon2. Store secrets in a dedicated vault. Encrypt sensitive columns at rest. Scan repositories and configs for hard-coded credentials.

entries.name: Injection — SQL Injection
entries.description: Unsanitised user input is concatenated into SQL queries, allowing attackers to read, modify, or delete database content.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Use parameterised queries or an ORM. Apply input validation and output encoding. Deploy a WAF as a secondary control. Run automated SQL injection scans in CI.

entries.name: Injection — OS Command Injection
entries.description: User-controlled input is passed to a system shell, enabling arbitrary command execution on the host.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Avoid shell invocation from user input entirely. If unavoidable, use an allowlist of safe arguments. Run services under restricted OS users with minimal permissions.

entries.name: Insecure Design — Missing Security Controls in Workflow
entries.description: Critical business workflows (e.g. password reset, multi-step checkout) lack threat-modelling-backed controls, allowing abuse.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Conduct threat modelling during design. Define security acceptance criteria per user story. Use reference implementations and security design patterns.

entries.name: Security Misconfiguration — Default Credentials in Production
entries.description: Software is deployed with unchanged default usernames and passwords, granting trivial access to attackers.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Require credentials to be changed on first deployment. Automate configuration hardening checks in the CI/CD pipeline. Include default-credential scanning in vulnerability assessments.

entries.name: Security Misconfiguration — Unnecessary Features Enabled
entries.description: Debug interfaces, sample applications, or unused features are enabled in production, expanding the attack surface.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Adopt infrastructure-as-code with a minimal base image. Disable all features not explicitly required. Run CIS Benchmark scans pre-deployment.

entries.name: Vulnerable and Outdated Components
entries.description: Third-party libraries, frameworks, or OS packages with known CVEs are used in production, enabling exploitation of public exploits.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Integrate software composition analysis (SCA) into CI. Subscribe to dependency vulnerability advisories. Enforce an SLA for patching critical CVEs within 72 hours.

entries.name: Identification and Authentication Failures — Weak Session Management
entries.description: Session tokens are guessable, not invalidated on logout, or transmitted over insecure channels, enabling session hijacking.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Generate cryptographically random session tokens. Invalidate all sessions on logout and password change. Enforce HTTPS. Apply appropriate session expiry and idle timeouts.

entries.name: Software and Data Integrity Failures — Insecure Deserialization
entries.description: The application deserialises untrusted data, allowing attackers to inject malicious objects and achieve remote code execution.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Never deserialise data from untrusted sources without integrity validation. Use safe serialisation formats (JSON with schema). Apply digital signatures or HMAC to serialised objects.

entries.name: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
entries.description: Security events (failed logins, access control violations) are not logged or monitored, preventing timely detection and response.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Log all authentication attempts, authorisation failures, and input validation errors. Centralise logs in a SIEM. Define alerting thresholds and test the detection pipeline regularly.

entries.name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
entries.description: The server fetches a URL supplied by the user, enabling attackers to reach internal systems or cloud metadata endpoints.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Validate and allowlist URLs before fetch operations. Block requests to RFC-1918 and link-local ranges at the network layer. Use an egress proxy for all outbound calls.

description: PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis) is a seven-stage, risk-centric threat-modeling methodology aligned with business objectives.

entries.name: Uncontrolled Third-Party Dependencies
entries.description: Business objectives rely on external vendors whose security posture is unknown, introducing unmanaged supply-chain risk.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enumerate all third-party dependencies and assess them against your risk criteria. Require security attestations (SOC 2, ISO 27001) from critical vendors. Maintain a software bill of materials (SBOM).

entries.name: Unprotected API Endpoints
entries.description: Publicly reachable API endpoints expose sensitive business functions without adequate authentication or rate-limiting controls.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Inventory all public-facing APIs with an API gateway. Enforce authentication (OAuth 2.0 / OIDC) and rate limiting on all endpoints. Perform automated API security scanning in CI.

entries.name: Chained Attack via Interconnected Services
entries.description: An attacker compromises one low-privilege service and chains vulnerabilities across interconnected microservices to reach high-value targets.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Adopt a zero-trust service mesh. Enforce strict inbound/outbound allow-lists per service. Simulate chained attack paths in tabletop exercises and adversarial testing.

entries.name: Business Logic Abuse
entries.description: An attacker exploits legitimate application features (e.g. discount codes, referral systems) in unintended ways to gain financial or data advantage.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Model abuse cases alongside normal use cases during design. Add anomaly detection for unusual business-logic patterns. Implement velocity checks for bulk or high-value operations.

entries.name: Unpatched Vulnerability in Business-Critical Component
entries.description: A known CVE in a business-critical component has not been remediated, providing attackers with a reliable public exploit.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Maintain a vulnerability management programme with SLAs based on CVSS severity and asset criticality. Use automated scanning and runtime protection (RASP) as compensating controls.

entries.name: Automated Credential Stuffing Attack
entries.description: Attackers use lists of compromised credentials from data breaches to automate login attempts and take over accounts at scale.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Enforce MFA. Integrate with breach-credential databases (e.g. HaveIBeenPwned API). Apply CAPTCHA and progressive delays after failed attempts. Alert on anomalous login volumes.

entries.name: Data Exfiltration Impact on Business Continuity
entries.description: Bulk exfiltration of customer or business data causes regulatory penalties, contractual breaches, and reputational damage.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Apply data-loss prevention (DLP) controls on outbound channels. Classify and tag sensitive data. Define and test an incident response and breach-notification plan.

description: LINDDUN is a privacy threat-modeling framework covering Linkability, Identifiability, Non-repudiation, Detectability, Disclosure of Information, Unawareness, and Non-compliance.

entries.name: Cross-Context Data Linkability
entries.description: Data items collected in separate contexts can be joined or correlated to build a detailed profile of an individual beyond their reasonable expectation.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Apply data minimisation. Use pseudonymous identifiers that differ per context. Evaluate whether data is linkable before collection. Conduct privacy impact assessments for new data flows.

entries.name: Re-identification via Quasi-Identifiers
entries.description: Supposedly anonymised datasets can be de-anonymised by combining quasi-identifiers (e.g. age, postcode, gender), revealing individuals' identities.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Apply k-anonymity, l-diversity, or differential privacy before releasing datasets. Assess re-identification risk formally before publishing. Restrict access to micro-data.

entries.name: Proof of Action (Non-repudiation of User Activity)
entries.description: Detailed system logs enable third parties (including the service provider) to prove that a specific user performed a sensitive action, undermining deniability.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Collect the minimum logging required for legitimate purposes. Separate content from metadata where possible. Retain logs only for the legally required minimum period.

entries.name: Presence Detectability via Traffic Analysis
entries.description: An observer can detect whether a user is using a sensitive service (e.g. an addiction helpline, a medical portal) by analysing encrypted traffic metadata.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Apply traffic shaping and constant-rate padding to obscure usage patterns. Use VPN or Tor-friendly designs for highly sensitive services. Avoid exposing distinct URL patterns per feature.

entries.name: Unauthorised Disclosure to Third Parties
entries.description: Personal data is shared with third-party analytics, advertising, or support tools embedded in the application without users' awareness or consent.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Audit all third-party SDKs and integrations for data sharing. Disclose third-party sharing in privacy notices. Obtain explicit consent where required. Use a consent management platform.

entries.name: Lack of Transparent Privacy Notice
entries.description: Users are unaware of how their data is collected, processed, or shared because privacy information is absent, incomplete, or inaccessible.
entries.suggested_mitigation: Provide a clear, layered privacy notice at point of collection. Use plain language and progressive disclosure. Present privacy information before or during data collection, not only in legal terms.

entries.name: Non-compliance with Data Subject Rights
entries.description: The system does not implement mechanisms enabling data subjects to exercise their GDPR/CCPA rights (access, erasure, portability, objection).
entries.suggested_mitigation: Build data subject rights into the product from inception. Implement an automated DSR workflow with defined SLAs. Keep an up-to-date record of processing activities (RoPA). Conduct periodic DPIA reviews.
